title: Traditional paper crane
subtitle: The classic orizuru, folding the Japanese paper crane from a square sheet
excerpt: The paper crane, or orizuru in Japanese, is the most recognised origami model in the world. It begins with a preliminary square base and a bird base from which wings, head, and tail emerge. A sequence of valley and mountain folds, consistent across centuries, produces the finished crane from any square of paper.
sourceNotes: The paper crane is a traditional Japanese origami model documented in public records from at least the Edo period (1603 to 1868 AD). The fold sequence described here follows the classical orizuru construction consistent across multiple historical and contemporary origami reference sources. No single modern author is the originator of this fold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold where the paper creases toward the folder, creating a valley or V-shape. The crease is on the upper surface of the paper after folding.
A fold where the paper creases away from the folder, creating a ridge or mountain shape. The crease is on the underside of the paper after folding; the fold is made by pushing the paper away.
A square base for many origami models: a flat square collapsed from diagonal and straight folds, giving a smaller square with four open flaps. The starting point for the paper crane.
An intermediate origami base produced by kite-folding and inside-reverse-folding a preliminary base. It has four narrow points from which the crane's head, tail, and two wings emerge.

aliases: japanese paper crane how to fold

content.text: The orizuru is the standard model for learning origami: it introduces the
preliminary base
and the
bird base
that underlie dozens of other traditional models. The fold sequence uses only
valley folds
and
mountain folds
and one inside-reverse fold. A 15 cm square of standard origami paper is the right starting size for a first attempt; thinner paper gives a lighter finished crane but is harder to fold precisely.
What you need

items.name: One square of paper, 15 cm × 15 cm
items.qty: 1 (origami paper, kami, or any thin square paper)

content.text: Making the preliminary base
Place the square coloured side down. Fold in half from bottom to top (valley fold). Unfold. Fold in half from left to right. Unfold. You have two crease lines crossing at the centre.

Turn the paper over (coloured side up). Fold diagonally from bottom-left corner to top-right corner (valley fold). Unfold. Fold diagonally from bottom-right corner to top-left corner. Unfold. You now have four crease lines: two straight and two diagonal.

Pick up the paper and bring the four corners toward each other, collapsing the sheet into a flat square with the coloured side outside. The diagonal creases become mountain folds; the straight creases become valley folds. Press flat. This is the preliminary base: a square with two open flaps on the right and two on the left.
Making the bird base

Hold the preliminary base with the open corner pointing down. Fold the lower-left edge of the top layer to the centre vertical crease (valley fold). Fold the lower-right edge of the top layer to the centre. The top layer now forms a kite shape with two new folds running from the open bottom corner to partway up the sides.
Fold the top triangular flap down over the two new folds, crease firmly, then unfold all three folds made in this step.

Lift the bottom open corner of the top layer and fold it upward along the creases made in steps 4 and 5, opening the two side folds as you go. The corner travels up to the top of the kite shape and the two side flaps fold inward, creating a long narrow diamond. Press flat. This is an inside-reverse fold.
Turn the model over. Repeat steps 4 through 6 on this side. The result is the bird base: four long narrow points, two on the left, two on the right, all meeting at a centre body.
Forming the crane

Fold the lower-left flap of the top layer up to the right (valley fold), as if turning a page. Fold the lower-right flap of the top layer up to the left. The model now shows two long points at the top (wings) and two at the bottom (neck and tail).
Turn the model over. Repeat: fold the lower-left flap to the right, and the lower-right flap to the left. Two wing tips point upward; two narrow points hang down.

Form the head: take one of the two lower points and make a small inside-reverse fold near the tip, bending the point slightly downward and forward. This becomes the beak. The other lower point is the tail.

Inflate the body: hold the crane by the neck and tail and gently pull the two apart. The body of the crane will puff outward. Ease the body open by pressing lightly inward at the sides. Pull the wings gently outward until the body is fully open and the wings spread flat.

items.symptom: The preliminary base does not collapse flat because the creases are wrong
items.cause: The straight folds should be valley folds and the diagonal folds should be mountain folds when collapsing, or vice versa, if the directions are reversed the collapse fights the paper rather than working with it.
items.fix: Make sure the diagonal creases are mountain folds (ridge pointing up) and the straight creases are valley folds (groove visible from above) before attempting the collapse. The paper will close into the base naturally when the crease directions are correct.

items.symptom: The body will not inflate when the neck and tail are pulled apart
items.cause: The inside folds of the body are too tightly creased, preventing the paper from opening.
items.fix: Before pulling the neck and tail, gently push each of the four walls of the body inward with a fingertip to loosen the creases. Then pull neck and tail slowly and evenly rather than sharply.
